core_contribution: A grammar-based object detection model for person detection that uses hierarchical deformable parts with variable structure and explicit occlusion modeling. The method introduces weak-label structural SVM for learning from weakly-labeled data (bounding boxes) while making strong predictions (detailed part configurations).
algorithm_steps: Step 1: Define grammar with start symbol Q having 6 production rules for different visibility patterns (heavy occlusion to no occlusion)
Step 2: For each part, define subtypes (left/right facing) and deformation schemas allowing quadratic displacement penalties
Step 3: Include explicit occlusion model O with appearance filters for occluding objects
Step 4: Add hierarchical subparts at multiple resolutions (same scale and 2x resolution)
Step 5: Train using weak-label structural SVM with loss function L(y,s) relating weak labels y to strong predictions s
Step 6: At test time, use dynamic programming to find maximum scoring derivations at each location
Step 7: Apply non-maximal suppression to produce final detections
key_equations: Eq 2: score(T) = sum of production scores + sum of terminal appearance scores
Eq 4: Deformation schema X(ω)^φ(δ) → {Y(ω)} with quadratic displacement features φ(δ) = (dx,dy,dx²,dy²)

key_assumptions.assumption: Person occlusion follows one of six discrete visibility patterns
key_assumptions.why_needed: Grammar structure requires finite set of production rules to model different occlusion states
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: Complex partial occlusions that don't match any of the six predefined patterns

key_assumptions.assumption: Part deformations follow quadratic displacement penalties
key_assumptions.why_needed: Enables efficient dynamic programming inference while modeling spatial flexibility
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: Highly articulated poses where quadratic penalties are insufficient to model joint constraints

key_assumptions.assumption: Occluding objects have consistent appearance that can be modeled with filters
key_assumptions.why_needed: Explicit occlusion model requires learnable appearance patterns for occluders
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: Occlusion by highly variable objects or transparent/reflective surfaces

baseline_limitations_identified: Previous systems use separate mixture components for different visibility patterns, dividing training data inefficiently and learning independent parts for each component
proposed_improvement: Single grammar model shares parts across visibility patterns, uses training data more efficiently, and includes explicit occlusion modeling with part subtypes for greater appearance variability
condition_where_baseline_wins: Simple scenes with clearly visible, unoccluded people where the additional complexity of grammar modeling provides no benefit over simpler mixture models
toy_dataset_justification: These datasets contain person detection tasks with varying degrees of occlusion and pose variation, suitable for testing hierarchical part models and occlusion handling
reproducibility_notes: Grammar structure is hand-designed and specific implementation details for weak-label structural SVM training are not fully specified. HOG feature extraction and dynamic programming inference are standard.

key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Models appearance of occluding objects when person is partially visible
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Reduced performance on partially occluded people, may increase false positives

key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Provides left/right facing variants for each part independently
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Less flexibility in modeling pose variation, reduced detection accuracy

key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Captures fine details at multiple resolutions for large objects
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Reduced performance on large person instances where fine details matter

known_failure_modes.scenario: Highly articulated poses not covered by the six visibility patterns
known_failure_modes.reason: Grammar structure is limited to predefined production rules
known_failure_modes.related_assumption: Person occlusion follows one of six discrete visibility patterns

known_failure_modes.scenario: Small objects where high-resolution subparts fall off feature pyramid
known_failure_modes.reason: Hierarchical model cannot use fine-scale features for very small detections
known_failure_modes.related_assumption: Part deformations follow quadratic displacement penalties
